hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Navigating Airports and Flights -- Check-in to Boarding (Consolidation)
lesson.menu_desc: A consolidation of the first conditional and request modals, at the airport: check-in, seat choice, boarding, and a gate change on the way to Lyon -- gate agent, overhead bin, aisle seat, boarding group, standby, jet bridge -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 11 -- Airports and Flights: Check-in to Boarding (Consolidation)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 11 -- Airports and Flights (Consolidation)

listenings.text: Good afternoon, and welcome to Terminal Four. This is a boarding announcement for flight nine zero four, with service to New York, John F. Kennedy. Please note that we have had a gate change. This flight will now depart from gate B twenty-two, not gate B fourteen as printed on your boarding pass. Again, that is a new gate: B twenty-two. If you need help finding it, any gate agent in a blue jacket will point you the right way. We will begin boarding in about twenty minutes. If you have a tight connection in New York, please come to the desk now and let us know, and we will do our best to help you. We will board by group, starting with passengers who need a little more time and our priority boarding members. When your boarding group is called, please have your boarding pass and your passport ready. Thank you for your patience, and we look forward to welcoming you on board.

Ladies and gentlemen, this is the final boarding call for flight twenty-two to Paris, Charles de Gaulle, now boarding at gate B twenty-two. All remaining passengers should make their way to the gate immediately, as the doors will close in ten minutes. If your name is on the standby list, please come to the desk. We have just released two seats, and we will call you in order. A quick reminder: only one carry-on and one small personal item are allowed in the cabin. If your bag does not fit in the overhead bin, our gate agent will check it through to Paris for free, and you can pick it up at baggage claim when you arrive. Passengers connecting in Paris, your next gate will be on the screen when you land, so there is no need to worry now. Once again, this is the final call for flight twenty-two to Paris. Please have your boarding pass ready as you come down the jet bridge. Thank you.

items.situation: Your carry-on is too big for the overhead bin, and the flight is full. Ask the gate agent what to do.
tips: Excuse me -- my carry-on won't fit in the overhead bin. Could you check it through to my final stop?
State the problem, then ask. The gate agent usually checks it for free when the flight is full.

items.situation: The gate agent says your flight is delayed and you might miss your connection in Paris. Ask what your options are, using a first conditional.
tips: If I miss my connection in Paris, what are my options? Will you rebook me on the next flight?
First conditional for the real risk: 'If I miss..., will you...?' Ask calmly, not in a panic.

items.situation: You were given a window seat, but you want an aisle seat. Ask the agent to change it.
tips: Is there any chance of an aisle seat instead? I'd really like to be able to get up on a long flight.
Ask, don't demand. 'Is there any chance of...?' is polite and gets a yes more often.

items.situation: The board shows a gate change to B22. Tell your travel companion what you both need to do.
tips: The gate changed -- if we hurry, we'll still make it. It's B22 now, not B14.
First conditional to plan: 'If we hurry, we'll make it.' Keep it short and calm.

items.situation: You're on the standby list for a full flight. Ask the gate agent how likely you are to get a seat.
tips: I'm on standby for this flight -- do you think a seat will open up? Should I stay near the gate?
'Do you think a seat will open up?' plus the modal 'Should I...?' -- exactly the right register.

items.situation: You have a twelve-hour layover in Shanghai on your way to a fair. Ask if you need a transit visa.
tips: I have a twelve-hour layover in Shanghai -- do I need a transit visa, or can I stay in the airport?
Ask before you fly. A long layover sometimes needs a transit visa; a short one usually doesn't.
